topic: Someone's childhood home is about to be demolished
raw_output:
[story 1]

The jackhammer's rhythmic thudding was a physical assault, vibrating up through the soles of my worn sneakers and settling deep in my chest. I stood by the overgrown lilac bush, gripping the rusted gate until my knuckles were white, watching the skeletal remains of the old porch sway in the wind. Every splintering crack of concrete felt like a personal accusation. I kept wiping a bead of sweat from my forehead with the back of my hand, the dampness chilling my skin despite the afternoon heat. *It’s ending,* a small, panicked voice whispered in my mind. I took a deep, shallow breath, trying to anchor myself to the solid ground beneath my feet, but my stomach churned like unsettled sand. I started pacing the perimeter of the property, my eyes darting from the demolition crew to the empty window frames, searching for any sign that this reality could somehow be rewritten.

[story 2]

"Just a few more hours," the foreman had said, his voice flat and utterly devoid of sympathy. But I couldn't hear him. All I could hear was the sickening crunch of wood giving way to dust. I was sitting on the warped steps of the front stoop, tracing patterns in the grime with my finger. My hands were trembling so badly I had to press them together tightly in my lap, the friction a small, desperate focus point. The air smelled of pulverized plaster and damp earth, a smell that clawed at my throat. I kept glancing toward the house—my house—as if expecting it to suddenly lurch, to collapse inward. My jaw was clenched so hard I tasted copper, and I refused to look at the heavy machinery, focusing instead on a single, stubborn weed pushing up through a crack in the driveway, a tiny, defiant green against the impending grey.

[story 3]

I kept replaying the blueprints in my head, trying to find a structural flaw, a loophole, anything to argue against the inevitable. The noise was relentless, a constant, grating symphony that drowned out all rational thought. My shoulders were hiked up near my ears, a rigid tension holding me upright against the sheer chaos unfolding before me. I watched a piece of molding tumble into a pile of rubble, and a sudden, sharp spike of anxiety shot through me, making my breath catch in my throat. I walked over to the foundation, running my fingertips along the cold, rough stone. It felt alien, hostile. I needed to move, needed to do *something* other than stand paralyzed, but my legs felt heavy, rooted to the spot as if gravity itself had increased its pull tenfold.
